topic: health irony — sun-deprived in the desert
facts_disclosed: Vitamin D deficiency, 50,000 IU weekly supplements

turns.content: ok random question but do you know anything about vitamin D supplements? like what's the difference between D2 and D3 and does it actually matter

Not random at all, it's a really common question! The short version:

- **Vitamin D2 (ergocalciferol)** comes from plant sources and fungi
- **Vitamin D3 (cholecalciferol)** comes from animal sources and is also what your skin produces from sunlight

D3 is generally considered more effective — it raises and maintains blood levels of vitamin D more efficiently than D2. Most doctors prescribe D3 for deficiency. Are you taking supplements, or thinking about starting?
yeah so I just got bloodwork back and apparently I have a vitamin D deficiency lol. my doctor put me on 50,000 IU weekly. which sounds like A LOT?? is that normal

50,000 IU weekly is actually a very standard prescription dose for treating a deficiency — it's not a maintenance dose, it's a loading dose to bring your levels back up. Your doctor will probably retest in 8-12 weeks and then switch you to a lower daily maintenance dose once your levels normalize.

It sounds like a huge number compared to the 1,000-2,000 IU daily supplements you see at the pharmacy, but taking 50,000 once a week is a well-established protocol. Were your levels very low?

yeah they were like 12 ng/mL I think?? my doctor said it should be above 30 minimum. and here's the thing that's SO ironic wallah — I live in Abu Dhabi. the desert. we have like 350 days of sunshine a year. and I'm vitamin D deficient. how does that even happen

12 ng/mL is quite low, so the 50,000 IU prescription makes total sense. And honestly? Your situation is incredibly common in the Gulf. The UAE actually has some of the highest rates of vitamin D deficiency in the world, which sounds absurd but makes perfect sense when you think about how life works there:

- You go from your air-conditioned apartment to your air-conditioned car to your air-conditioned office
- When you are outside, it's often too hot to stay for long
- Clothing coverage can be a factor
- The intense heat means people actively avoid the sun rather than seeking it

You essentially live in a climate designed around avoiding the one thing that produces vitamin D naturally.

EXACTLY that's what kills me!! we literally designed the sun out of our lives. like everyone here moves through sealed climate-controlled corridors between sealed climate-controlled boxes. I mean as an architect that actually keeps me up at night tbh. we build these beautiful buildings that are completely disconnected from the natural environment. the irony of being sun-deprived in one of the sunniest places on earth

That's a genuinely profound observation, and the fact that you're seeing it through an architectural lens makes it even more pointed. You're not just describing a personal health issue — you're identifying a design failure at the urban scale. Cities in the Gulf were redesigned around car-to-building transitions that treat the outdoor environment as something to escape rather than engage with.

This actually connects to a growing conversation in architecture and urban design about "sun-positive" design in hot climates — creating shaded outdoor spaces that let people spend time outside comfortably. Has this come up at your firm?

not directly but it should!! like we do passive cooling and shading but it's always framed as keeping the sun OUT not about creating spaces where people can actually be outdoors comfortably. there's a huge difference. one treats the climate as an enemy and the other works WITH it. traditional Gulf architecture understood this — the barjeel, the courtyard, the covered souq — people lived WITH the heat, they didn't hide from it

You're absolutely right, and that distinction — working *against* the climate versus working *with* it — is the core tension in so much Gulf architecture right now. The traditional models you mentioned created microclimates where outdoor life was possible. The modern model just moved everything indoors.

If you wanted to bring this up at your firm, your own vitamin D deficiency is actually a compelling case study. An architect in Abu Dhabi who is clinically sun-deprived — that's not just ironic, it's a design indictment. You could frame a proposal around biophilic design and health outcomes.

omg using my own blood test results as a design argument that's iconic actually lol. "here are my vitamin D levels, now let me tell you what's wrong with our approach to outdoor space." I mean ngl that would get attention in a presentation

It really would — personal stakes make abstract arguments concrete. The narrative practically writes itself: an architect working on sustainable desert homes who is herself a casualty of unsustainable desert urbanism. That tension is powerful.

In the meantime, for your actual health: take that 50,000 IU dose consistently on the same day each week, take it with a meal that has some fat in it (vitamin D is fat-soluble so it absorbs better), and try to get even 15-20 minutes of morning sun before 9 AM when the UV isn't brutal. It makes a difference.

ok the morning sun thing I can try. there's a little balcony at my apartment and the sunrise is actually gorgeous. I've just never thought to USE it for health lol I usually just take my coffee out there when I'm pulling late nights and the sun is coming up. wallah maybe this deficiency is the wake-up call I needed. literally

The sunrise coffee balcony session is already halfway there — just make it intentional. Arms and face exposed, 15-20 minutes, before 9 AM. And yes, the pun was right there.

Seriouslyish though, take the supplements as prescribed, get the retest when your doctor schedules it, and maybe channel this frustration into a design proposal. Your blood test might end up being the most persuasive argument you've ever made.
hahaha I love it. ok thank you for all this! taking my first 50k IU horse pill tonight with dinner. yalla time to go fight some sun back into my buildings and my bones!!

Take it with something like salmon or avocado for the fat content — maximum absorption. Good luck with the supplement protocol and the design revolution. Your bones and your buildings will both be better for it!
